data.title: ImmunityBio: Taking Care Of Business
data.description:
It has been roughly three months since my last ImmunityBio, Inc. (NASDAQ:IBRX) article, where I discussed the company's $100M public offering, which I thought was necessary to support the company's commercialization efforts for Anktiva and keep their pipeline programs moving forward. Despite my concerns about the company's cash burn rate and long-term financial health, I remained bullish on the ticker due to Anktiva's strong performance and upcoming milestones, including a permanent J-code and regulatory filings in the UK and EU.

Admittedly, I have discounted the ongoing BCG shortage as a serious near-term risk for ImmunityBio because the product's commercial growth is unlikely to instantly outgrow the market. However, the demand for BCG has dramatically outgrown the supply, which will ultimately cap Anktiva's growth in non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC). Thankfully, ImmunityBio is looking to help address the BCG shortage with their recombinant BCG ((rBCG)) and is ready to hit the market thanks to the FDA authorizing an Expand Access Program. It looks as if the company taking care of business and is attempting to address one of the primary limiting agents for Anktiva's growth. As a result, I am looking to add to my IBRX position in anticipation the company's recent efforts will drive significant revenue growth and conserve their cash runway.

I intend to provide an update on ImmunityBio and the ongoing concerns over the global supply of BCG. Then, I will discuss the company's rBCG efforts and how they could help improve Anktiva's commercial outlook. In addition, I will highlight some downside risks that investors should consider when managing their IBRX position. Finally, I take a look at the charts in an attempt to identify an opportunity to add to my IBRX position.

Recent Updates

ImmunityBio started off 2025 announcing plans for multiple FDA submissions this year, including a sBLA for ANKTIVA for BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) with papillary disease. In addition, ImmunityBio will submit a BLA for ANKTIVA in 2nd and 3rd non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) following a failure with a checkpoint inhibitor. Subsequently, ImmunityBio publicized a collaboration with BeiGene (ONC) for this confirmatory trial using the latter's tislelizumab.

In February, the company noted that the FDA authorized expanded access to the rBCG source. The company has also highlighted that their global regulatory submissions for Anktiva in BCG-unresponsive NMIBC have been accepted by MHRA and EMA. Moreover, the FDA granted RMAT designation for Anktiva and CAR-NK (PD-L1 t-haNK) in combination with chemo or radiotherapy for reversing lymphopenia for metastatic pancreatic cancer.

March was also a big month for ImmunityBio. The company started off the month revealing that the permanent J-Code awarded at the beginning of the year, which has helped Anktiva's sales accelerate, with unit sales volume in February increasing 67% month-over-month from January and overall 2025 sales to that point have surpassed Q4's total sales. ImmunityBio also conveyed that their Lynch Syndrome trial is moving along well, with over 100 participants being dosed with Anktiva.

The company's most recent press release announced the first U.S. dosing of their rBCG under the FDA's EAP. ImmunityBio publicized that Urology Partners, one of the country's largest independent urology providers, was among one of the first to offer rBCG as an option. The company highlighted that there are over 60 sites in the process of employing rBCG amid TICE BCG supply constraints.

So, it looks as if ImmunityBio has made significant progress on the clinical, regulatory, and commercial fronts since the beginning of 2025. Not only should these labors help in the near term, but they ought to have a dramatic impact in the coming years Anktiva amasses more indications, and the company continues to expand their reach outside the United States.

BCG Discussion

The severe shortage of TICE BCG has been a major in oncology because it is the standard-of-care intravesical therapy for NMIBC. Merck (MRK) is the sole supplier of TICE BCG in the U.S., and they have struggled to meet the demand, which has led to urologists having to ration access to this critical therapy. This shortage has also been a major concern for ImmunityBio and IBRX investors because Anktiva's label is for use with BCG for NMIBC patients who were unresponsive to BCG. The concern there is that Anktiva needs BCG to be used as a treatment, and they also need patients to have previously tried and failed to respond to BCG as a monotherapy. So, ImmunityBio is currently being restricted in two ways by the BCG shortage… supply to be used with Anktiva, and eligible patients.

As I mentioned in my introduction, I did see the BCG shortage to be an overwhelming risk for ImmunityBio immediately after launch, but it was clear that it was going to be the limiting agent at some point. Considering the company's clinical, regulatory, and commercial progress, it looks like their collaboration with the Serum Institute of India to manufacture a next-gen rBCG. Not only could the rBCG bolster the supply of BCG, but also potentially improve treatment efficacy and safety. In fact, the rBCG has already undergone Phase II clinical trials in Europe, where it posted impressive immunogenicity and improved safety matched to earlier BCG formulations. So, beyond simply filling the supply gap, ImmunityBio's combination of rBCG and Anktiva could potentially become the new standard-of-care and remodel the NMIBC treatment paradigm.

Anktiva Outlook

Now with ImmunityBio working with the FDA for rBCG EAP, I believe the company has an improved commercial outlook for 2025 and beyond. Even before the FDA's decision, Anktiva was recording strong commercial momentum with unit sales up 97% in December, and their Q1 sales have already exceeded all of Q4 of 2024. With the potential expansion in NMIBC and pending approvals in the UK and EU, I would expect Anktiva's NMIBC revenue to easily reach $80M in 2025, and a conservative estimate of $100M in 2026. However, the unknown would be how much the rBCG sales would contribute over the next few years. The global BCG market is projected to be over $600M in 2023, so I can see rBCG generating $25M in 2025, with a potential upside beyond $75M in 2026 if they get full approval. So, that would be roughly $105M for 2025 and over $175M for 2026.

Looking at the Street's revenue estimates, we can see that they have a similar outlook for 2025 with roughly $105M in revenue. However, analysts have a more bullish outlook for 2026 with nearly $300M in annual revenue.

So, perhaps the Street is expecting a big bump from rBCG and possible European sales next year. The company did report that:

In a recent Sermo survey of 100 U.S. urologists, 57 percent indicated they were unable to treat patients in the last 12 months due to a lack of access to TICE BCG...

...so perhaps they are anticipating a strong surge in the near-term. Looking further out, I will point to ImmunityBio's other promising pipeline programs to be big growth drivers into the next decade. It looks as if analysts are projecting ImmunityBio to report explosive growth and will pull in roughly $4.65B in 2030, which would be 0.54x forward price-to-sales.

Considering the size of the company's target markets, I can see ImmunityBio crossing the $1B market in short order. That level of growth should have a dramatic impact on the EPS, and the company maximizes Anktiva true potential. Analysts are projecting that the company to report a positive EPS for 2027, and will continue to report double-digit and triple-digit growth into the next decade.

If these projections become reality, ImmunityBio would quickly become one of the major players in immunotherapeutics and likely one of the fastest-growing biotechs on the Street. Certainly, we would have to expect this level of growth would have a major impact on the share price. Indeed, it is hard to fathom how ImmunityBio can go from pulling in several million a quarter in sales to recording several billion in annual sales in only a few years, but that is what Anktiva's commercial potential could deliver. A reminder, the company is slated for multiple clinical and regulatory catalysts this year that could improve the likelihood of the company hitting the Street's estimate in the coming years.

Downside Risks

Although the company is projected to report strong growth in the coming quarters and years, the company's financial health remains a major concern. Taking a look at the company's balance sheet, it looks as if ImmunityBio has a quarterly cash burn of $104.4M. The company's December 2024 cash balance was $149.8M, so the current cash runway appears to be around 1.25 quarters or less than 4 months.

Indeed, the company is expected to report significant revenue growth this quarter, which could help offset the burn rate by maybe a week or two, assuming expenses don't scale aggressively. Therefore, it is possible the company could run out of funds in the coming weeks, thus forcing the company to perform some fundraising in order to keep the company running.

In addition, ImmunityBio has several clinical and regulatory risks as their pipeline programs continue to move deeper into development and onto the market. Any major setback could have a dramatic impact on the share price as investors anticipate additional cash burn as revenue is delayed and the potential costs to address the issue.

Considering these risks, I am maintaining my IBRX conviction level of 3 out of 5. The ticker will remain a "Top Idea" in the "Bio Boom" speculative portfolio in the Compounding Healthcare Investing Group.

Looking For a Buy

Despite the company's commercial progress, the market continues to punish IBR, with the share price struggling to hold $3 per share. It seems like every positive update is followed by concerns of finances and fears of dilution. This has forced me to remain patient and keep my IBRX position in a "House Money" status. However, the catalysts slated for 2025 could have a dramatic impact on the company's long-term outlook. In addition, Anktiva's growth potential might be able to outpace the cash burn in a couple of years.

Therefore, I am keeping an eye on IBRX to see if I can identify a high-conviction reversal setup to add to my dormant position. Looking at the Daily Chart, we can see IBRX is trading below my Buy Level 1 of $3.40 per share, which is where I am willing to increase my share sizing and decrease my selectivity for technical metrics. Well, considering IBRX is trading below all my key Anchored-VWAPs, finding a Grade A reversal setup is unlikely in the near term. That being said, we can see a bullish divergence in the RSI along with a higher-low being formed with the share price, so, perhaps we are starting to see some ingredients needed for a reversal in the coming weeks.

Taking a closer look at the Daily Chart, we can see how the share price was able to break the downtrend ray that originated in October 2024. In addition, the share price is battling the downtrend ray from March 28 of this year. If the ticker can get above the anchored VWAPS from the proximal highs and proximal lows, that could scare off the bears for the time being and allow the ticker to test the 200-Day EMA (~$3.80). So, I will be on the lookout in the coming days to see if the share price is able to break above these VWAPs before committing to a buy.

On the other hand, I am not going to make any substantial additions until the company is able to address their cash position. Once the cash position is addressed, I will continue to make periodic additions as long as the ticker rests below my Buy Threshold of $4.00 per share. Once I have my fill, I will set a sell order around my Sell Target 1 of $9.00 per share with the aim of booking some profit and moving my position closer to a "house money" status. In the long term, I intend to trade IBRX for at least five more years with the expectation that the company will maximize ANKTIVA's commercial potential, and the market will start valuing the ticker properly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Canadian Pacific Kansas City: Still Less Compelling Than Treasuries
data.description:
It’s been about three months since I suggested that the shares of Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (NYSE:CP) were more expensive than the S&P 500 in an article with the magnificently original title “Canadian Pacific Kansas City: Twice as Expensive as the S&P 500.” Since then, the shares have returned a loss of about 4% against a loss of about 7% for the S&P 500. I should also point out that I suggested that investors put their capital to work in a Treasury Note, rather than this stock. So, while the shares of CP were returning a negative 4%, the 10-Year Treasury Note is up about 4% since then. Those who know me best know that I have a long and varied list of terrible personality flaws. Pettiness is one of them, and so I have to admit that this 8%+ outperformance of the Treasury Note pleases me greatly.

Anyway, the company has released their annual results, so I thought I’d review the name yet again to see if it’s now worth buying. I’ll make that determination by reviewing the financial results, and, once again, by looking at how this investment stacks up against what the finance nerds call “The Risk Free Rate.” If the stock doesn’t offer the prospects of a superior return when compared to the risk-free alternative, it’s not worth investing in in my view.

I know that my readers are the most interesting people on this forum, who live the most dynamic and meaningful lives. I imagine you’re planning your next exotic vacation, or working out which supermodel you’re going to spend the weekend with. For my part, I’ve got hours of “The Young & The Restless” to catch up on. The point is, we all have busy lives, which means we don’t have time to either “dilly” or “dally” with lengthy articles. It’s for this reason that I offer a thesis statement at the beginning of each of my articles. This gives you the chance to read my thoughts in a nutshell, thus obviating the need to be exposed to too much “Doyle mojo.” You’re welcome. I was pleasantly surprised by the financial results here. The problem is that the Treasury Note remains a much more compelling investment, despite the drop in yield. The dividend would need to grow at a CAGR of about 35.5% to simply match the cash flows of the Treasury Note, meaning that capital appreciation needs to do the “heavy lifting.” The problem with that idea is the fact that the stock price is lower today than it was four years ago. I therefore still prefer Treasuries.

Financial Snapshot

If you’re in the mood to focus on the income statement, you would think that the company had a very good year. Revenue and operating income were up by 15.8%, and 18% respectively. “That’s not nothing”, as the young people say. If you’re more of a “glass half empty” kind of guy, as I often am, you’d be inclined to focus more on the balance sheet. Long-term debt is up another $129 million over the past year, which helps explain why interest expense is up by about $30 million, or 3.9% when compared to 2023. Additionally, there are about 1.285 million more shares outstanding than there were this time last year. These are relatively minor problems in the grand scheme of things, though. I think the financial performance here was reasonably good, and so I’d be willing to buy the shares if I can acquire them at a reasonable price, and if they offer a significant risk-adjusted return when compared to the 10-Year Treasury Note.

Stock Vs. Treasury Note

In my view, in the domain of investing, everything's relative. That means that we judge one investment against the host of other investments that are available to us. If we're money managers with a strict mandate, we have a very limited number of choices. Most of us are retail investors who can invest in anything, and so we compare a given stock to the universe of potential options, including precious metals, bonds, commodities, etc. Second, we're not seeking high returns. We're instead seeking the highest risk-adjusted returns available to us from the universe of investments we can choose.

Given the above, I want to compare every stock that I might buy to the 10-Year Treasury Note. If I can take on less risk and earn a higher return on a government bond than I would from a given stock, why would I buy the stock? In particular, I would like to compare the cash flows from each investment and work out the rate at which the stock dividend will need to grow to simply match the cash flows of the Treasury. Please note that as of April 3, 2025, the yield on the 10-Year Treasury Note is about 4.04%, and the TTM dividend yield here is about 0.75%. Please note, I don't use "forward" dividend yields because they involve too much speculation in my view. I like to anchor on the known.

I present the results of that question below for your enjoyment and edification. It turns out that the dividend here will need to grow at about 35.5% to simply match the cash flows that an investor will definitely receive from the 10-Year Treasury Note. I don’t like to make definitive statements on this forum, but that will not happen. Thus, the 10-Year Treasury Note will generate more cash flow than will the stock.

That means, obviously, that to match the returns of the Treasury Note, the stock will have to rise in price over the next decade at an extraordinary rate. Consider that the stock price is about 9% lower today than it was exactly four years ago, and the idea of capital appreciation “picking up the slack”, as the young people say, seems fanciful.

So, I’m of the view that investors who are interested in maximizing their risk-adjusted returns should eschew these shares. I am of the view that it’s almost inevitable that they will offer lower risk-adjusted returns than Treasuries over the next decade. Also, I think it’s worth remembering that the shares of a company should offer superior returns to an asset with perfect predictability like a Treasury, which highlights how relatively unattractive this stock is.

Risks To My Preference for Treasuries & My Response

Please understand that I know that Treasuries are not technically “risk-free.” In particular, the risk of inflation hangs heavy over these investments. To that, I would point out that inflation is a tax on all investments, including shares of Canadian Pacific Kansas City. Another potential criticism of my approach might be that this railroad owns assets that are in great hedges against inflation. That sounds plausible enough until you remember the “9% lower today than it was four years ago” statement above. Those of you paying attention at home may remember that inflation has been troublesome over the past few years, yet these shares are lower. This suggests to me that the market is unaware of, or rejects their hedge value. Given the above, I consider the “Treasuries are subject to inflation” argument to be a wash, and that government debt offers superior risk-adjusted returns to this investment.

data.title: WeRide: Not Likely Riding Higher For Now
data.description:
WeRide Inc. (NASDAQ:WRD) offers all the promises of autonomous vehicles, but the business still has limited results and a difficult-to-understand business model. The Chinese company has a lot of opportunities around the globe, but the robotaxis business is limited, and the international expansion is mostly in small countries. My investment thesis is more Neutral on the stock.

Minimal Robotaxis

WeRide has a lot of first-mover advantages in AVs with work in areas like Abu Dhabi and Switzerland, but neither country amounts to a lot of revenue potential. The company just announced an expansion in Abu Dhabi with plans to have 50 robotaxis operating on the Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) ridesharing platform by mid-year along with plans to launch services in Dubai starting up soon.

The company claims to have L4 vehicles deployed in 30 cities in 10 countries around the globe, including 20 cities in China including Beijing. WeRide operates robotaxis, a robobus, a robosweeper and a robovan in China offering diversification not seen by other robotaxi operators.

While WeRide has all of these operations, the company has limited actual revenues. The robotaxi business reported Q4 sales of only $19 million with just $7 million from Product revenues tied to the sales from robotaxis, robosweepers, and robovans. Total sales actually declined 3% YoY.

The primary revenue line actually declined due to a reduction in ADAS R&D and technical support services. The Services revenues were $12 million in the quarter.

WeRide generates revenues from international locations via selling AVs, revenue sharing, and fees. The company expects to generate high-margin revenues from the start, but naturally won't participate much in the recurring revenues from operating a robotaxi service.

The Chinese market is where WeRide will focus on operating the robotaxi service called WeRide Go to generate attractive recurring fare revenues, but the market currently has extremely low rates.

Depending on the report, the overall global AV market is estimated in the trillions. WeRide lists a report targeting a $1.75 trillion market opportunity just in 2030, which would be enormous growth for a market leader like this company.

The issue remains exactly how WeRide is going to profit from this opportunity. The company has listed robobus and robovan orders in the 10,000+ unit range, but the latest quarterly results provide no real indication of delivery volumes in the years ahead.

On the Q4 '24 earnings call, analyst Tim Hsiao from Morgan Stanley asked specifically about the business model as follows (emphasis added):

Tim Hsiao

So, the first question is about WeRide's and its business model, because we noticed the industry has been evolving rapidly. So, we definitely want to know that. What is WeRide's current Robotaxi business model?

Jennifer Li

Thank you, Tim. I'll take the first question. Our Robotaxi business model is quite unique from others. For international markets outside China, we provide autonomous vehicles and services to local partners and global platforms through a combination of selling the vehicles, charging a fixed service fee, and a revenue-sharing arrangement. This ensures we have a positive contribution margin from day one. Our strategy focuses on collaborating with leading platforms in each market, so that we can achieve faster Robotaxi deployment, better utilization of Robotaxi fleet, and stronger pricing power.

The company just doesn't make it clear how the business will benefit from the 50-vehicle plan for Uber in Abu Dhabi. Over time, WeRide targets 60% margins in these international locations.

Revenue Ramp Questions

The stock has a listed market cap in the $4 billion range. The business is still not very well-developed considering the licenses to operate robotaxis in 4 countries.

WeRide does have a large cash balance of $670 million after the big IPO last year, along with another $239 million in financial assets. The company reported an adjusted loss of $110 million last year.

The consensus analyst estimates only have revenues reaching above $100 million this year. The big story is whether WeRide can actually reach the revenue targets topping $400 million in 2026, though the AV market definitely has the potential to reach an inflection point where sales soar for the rest of the decade.

The stock has an enterprise value closer to only $3 billion, but the cash balance won't contribute much to the stock valuation as long as WeRide is burning a lot of cash. The stock already trades close to 10x 2026 sales targets and the stock market will likely need better clarification on the business model and the revenue ramp before WeRide gets a more premium valuation.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that WeRide has a massive opportunity ahead in the autonomous driving market. The company faces big questions on monetizing this massive opportunity leaving our view cautious on the stock for now.

data.title: Alto Ingredients Should Be Sold To Preserve Shareholder Value
data.description:
A year ago, I wrote an article on Alto Ingredients, Inc. (NASDAQ:ALTO) called "If Alto Ingredients Couldn't Make Money In 2023, It Likely Won't In 2024 Either." Despite some lively debate from bulls opining otherwise, it turned out that the company lost another $60 million for 2024 and is currently making new 52-week and all-time lows. Rumblings from activist shareholders and churn on the Board of Directors has had little impact to the sentiment on the stock. Shareholders, fellow Seeking Alpha analyst Henrik Alex and neutral market observers are all saying it's time for the company to consider either an asset sale or a complete sale of the company. I happen to agree with that suggestion. Every day that ALTO exists as a standalone entity is a day that erodes value for shareholders.

It's gotten to the point where even ALTO management admits that asset sales or some strategic transaction may be a necessary course of action. ALTO undertook fairly aggressive cost savings initiatives during Q4, but in my opinion this is too little, too late. The time to do this was last year. When that course of action might have eked out a small profit or given the company's stock price some momentum. That would enable it to negotiate a buyout from a position of relative strength.

2024: Another $55 Million of Shareholder's Equity Eroded

A review of ALTO's 2024 annual report shows that shareholder's equity declined $55 million from $280 million to $225 million year-over-year. Working capital eroded $9 million from $104 million to $95 million, while the company's long-term debt increased $11 million from $82 million to $93 million. While the erosion of the financials isn't at a level of severity that precludes ALTO from maximizing the value of its assets had it sold at the optimal time, the optimal time was 2023 or 2024. Not so much in 2025. As debt will likely be larger than the working capital by the time the next financial report comes out, time is running out for ALTO to wait for the opportune time to complete a transaction.

My recent analysis on REX American Resources Corporation (NYSE:REX) shows that while I have actually turned bullish on that particular company, industry sentiment, crush margins and other macro forces for 2025 will settle decidedly below the boom period from mid-2023 into 2024 for ethanol producers. Alto has shifted away from being purely an ethanol play into a variety of alcohol and agricultural ingredients, but REX remains a good bellwether. If REX is expected to show a significant decline in earnings in 2025, there is no chance that Alto is going to suddenly turn it around despite the cost-cutting measures, in my opinion.

Analysts are forecasting a -$0.30 EPS for 2025. If this operating loss is funded out of working capital, I estimate a $23 million erosion to $70 million by the end of 2025.

ALTO Still Has Potential Value and Upside, But Must Act Quickly

Not all is lost for ALTO shareholders. ALTO somehow scores an A rating on Seeking Alpha's valuation analysis. Though this grade is a value trap once you take a deeper look into each metric:

Due to its position of perpetual losses, ALTO doesn't produce meaningful earnings multiples and is therefore given a free pass on what otherwise would be performance well below the sector average. Its A ranking comes from having extremely cheap revenue and book value multiples, a function of the eroding stock price. That being said, there comes a point when it no longer makes any sense to sell the stock. Certain companies in dying or saturated industries such as big box retail or busted electric vehicle science experiments have to make peace with the fact that their assets have little to no value. Those investments could always go to zero in a bankruptcy situation. So, for shareholders, it's better to collect something than nothing. ALTO shareholders aren't in that position, at least not yet. The company merely miserably fails at being able to profitably monetize its valuable assets. Regardless of macro impacts on the industry.

While I was right about being bearish on ALTO in that past, I don't take pleasure from seeing bulls continue to lose money on this stock. They have every right to push for a strategic transaction and/or a change in leadership to try to maximize their investment. If my article can be one small drop in the ocean in favor of that movement, I am willing to write it.

As I mentioned before, shareholder's equity is $225 million. That likely erodes to just over $200 million by the end of the year. ALTO's current market cap is $90 million. While bulls would argue that the assets are worth substantially more than what is listed at cost on the balance sheet, I would counter that they are not negotiating from a position of strength. Any purchase price at book value or higher should be considered a win at this point in time. That would lead to a 2-3x upside from the current stock price.

As for an investment thesis, I am not touching this stock with a ten-foot poll. The upside is not nearly enough for me to get excited about it, given the risk profile. However, for someone who has already accumulated a large position and is deep in the red, it doesn't make much sense to sell at this level either. The best course of action is to throw support behind any activist investors who are looking to maximize ALTO's value. Therefore, I am upgrading my opinion on this stock from sell to hold as I think there will be some level of success in pursuing a strategic transaction in the future. But that future better come sooner than later because time is not on the side of shareholders as ALTO continues to put up losses every quarter.

data.title: Gold Tumbles $100+, $3050 Support Holds Firm. What Next?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices fell off a cliff this morning despite a weaker US Dollar, as tariffs propelled the precious metal to fresh all-time highs overnight. The accelerated selloff could in part be down to profit taking following a sharp rise to $3167/oz before the Asian session began.

President Donald Trump's tariff announcement did shake markets last night, but as other havens such as the JPY and CHF have gained today, Gold has struggled. This was a possibility that I had covered in my Gold piece earlier in the week titled

What comes next for Gold prices?

Gold had an aggressive selloff, breaking initial support at the 3085 handle before faltering at 3053. The bounce from near the 3050 support handle has itself been aggressive, as Gold rallied to a high of 3093 at the time of writing.

The volatility is to be expected, with more uncertainty gripping markets and haven demand present. I do expect gold to hold firm and potentially extend its recovery.

The lack of any clear catalyst for the selloff leads me to believe that it is largely down to profit taking and repositioning post the tariff announcement.

Tariffs impact on Gold

These tariffs are likely to raise concerns about global economic growth and create uncertainty about how other countries might respond, which could keep supporting gold prices.

On the bright side, the announcement brought some relief to metal markets. It’s now clear that steel, aluminum, gold, and copper imports won’t be affected by these tariffs. For gold, this exemption might lead to a slowdown in gold imports into the US.

The week will end with the US Nonfarm Payrolls (NFP) report for March, coming out on Friday. This key jobs report will influence what the market expects from the Federal Reserve's future policies. Earlier this week, the ADP Employment Change report showed the private sector added 155,000 jobs in March, well above the forecast of 105,000 and February's 84,000.

Given all the narrative in play, I still do expect bullish pressure to remain on Gold prices. The clarity needed for this to not be an issue is yet to arrive and thus I can see Gold prices rising to test recent highs in the days ahead.

Given the scenarios presented above, the week ahead looks to be a volatile one with a lot of whipsaw price action ahead.

Overall, recent developments mean that the bulls remain in charge. What we have seen of late is that when it comes to Gold, overall market sentiment seems to be the biggest driving force.

Technical Analysis - Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical analysis standpoint, Gold prices have whipsawed today with a $100 drop.

The daily chart below was extremely bearish at one point, but given a $70-odd rally to the upside, the risk was muted.

For a more longer term outlook in Gold, read: Gold (XAU/USD)price analysis: Gold Surges but have "liberation day" tariffs been priced in?

Gold (XAU/USD) April 3, 2025

Dropping down to a two-hour timeframe, as you can see, the support level around 3083 held firm even though the price printed a low of 3053.

The two hour-candle close with a large downside wick and aggressive bounce are with a doubt a significant amount of bullish pressure.

Immediate resistance rests a 3125 and 3150 before all-time highs at 3167 come into focus.

On the support front, immediate support rests at 3100 before the 3083 and 3053 handles come into play.

  • 3050

data.title: Palantir: Building A Monopoly With Powerful Data Flywheel
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) is not just another company riding the AI wave. Rather, it is becoming an integral part of the wave itself. The company finds itself at the intersection of big data, critical operations, and AI, leveraging a virtuous cycle that is built on a unique self-reinforcing data advantages. Palantir's core insight is that its proprietary data, gathered from real-world information flowing through its business and government clients, compounds in value when harnessed at scale. Every additional customer acquisition adds fuel to Palantir's exponential data moat, which is becoming a large differentiator in a world of commoditized AI models.

The Self-Reinforcing Data Flywheel

Palantir's greatest long-term advantage arguably lies in its self-reinforcing data flywheel. At the heart of its major platforms like Foundry and Gotham is the integration of vast pools of proprietary customer data. Palantir does not just passively store customer information, but rather actively learns from it. This allows Palantir to optimize workflows and refine analytical models for the customer's unique needs. As a growing number of users within an organization rely on Palantir for decisions, from supply chain logistics to intelligence analysis, they generate even more data. This in turn makes Palantir's insights even sharper, which would in turn drive even more usage.

This result leads to a compounding cycle. The higher usage yields more data, more data yields smarter software, and smarter software attracts even more usage. This virtuous cycle gives Palantir a lead in real-world proprietary data that is incredibly hard for newcomers to catch. After years of gathering data from some of the world's most complex data sets, Palantir has enough of a lead that generic providers likely will not be able to match. Each new deployment of its platforms not only delivers immediate value, but also feeds back improvements to Palantir's broader ecosystem. This widens the data moat with every iteration.

Increasing AI Commoditization is a Major Tailwind

There appears to be a trend in AI where even the most powerful models are being commoditized at a shockingly fast rate. While this has negative implications for companies directly involved in the business building and selling models, this trend of commoditization is beneficial for Palantir. Palantir saw this coming and built its strategy around a simple truth. When algorithms are ubiquitous, it is the proprietary data and the ability to operationalize AI that become the true differentiators. Palantir focuses on being the indispensable layer that connects AI to real-world data and decisions.

Palantir's value proposition only grows as foundation model innovations proliferate. After all, clients rely on Palantir to integrate the latest AI breakthroughs into their own data ecosystems behind secure firewalls. In a world of constantly evolving and changing frontier models, Palantir's services will only grow in demand. In effect, AI commoditization has shifted the power to those who control the data and deployment infrastructure, which is exactly where Palantir thrives. Palantir acts as the last mile for AI applications, where raw algorithms turn into impactful solutions.

Mission-Critical Infrastructure

Palantir continues to rely on product-led growth grounded in real-world impact. The company's platforms are already embedded in critical operations across both industry and government. This can be anything from coordinating military intelligence to streamlining vaccine distribution in hospitals. The use cases of Palantir's products are near-endless. Unsurprisingly, Palantir also powers data-driven decision-making at an increasingly diverse array of Fortune 500 companies, including firms in manufacturing, finance, and beyond.

A great measure of Palantir's effectiveness is how quickly usage tends to spread within an organization. Analysts, engineers, and executives all start to rely on Palantir's unified data environment to collaborate and make better decisions. The result is products that are incredibly sticky, as it is hard to rip out or replace a platform that essentially becomes the nervous system of an enterprise. What's more, the rapid improvements in AI only make Palantir's platforms increasingly vital to organizations as time passes.

Palantir has also started to hit a distribution inflection point within the commercial sector, with its customer count jumping 43% in the last year alone. More impressive is the fact that its commercial revenue of $803 million actually increased 134% YoY, hinting at higher per capita usage. Palantir has clearly done a good job of transforming from a niche, high-touch solution for government contractors to a more generalized platform that can benefit organizations of all types.

Large Risks and Challenges Remain

The most obvious concern facing Palantir is its heavy reliance on government contracts. If public priorities shift, the company's heavy dependence on defense and intelligence work could hurt growth. In addition, the unpredictable nature of the new administration will only add a greater degree of volatility to Palantir. However, the risk is continually easing as the commercial segment is now driving the majority of revenue. In fact, U.S. commercial sales grew 64% YoY in Q4 of 2024, which derisks Palantir by balancing its customer mix.

Competition is also a growing threat, as a large number of startups and large players alike are pitching data platforms and AI solutions to enterprises. While Palantir does have a data edge based on the fact that the company has been in the industry longer than most, it is possible for larger companies to operate at a loss in order to catch up to Palantir. There are plenty of companies with enough resources to do so. However, such companies will lack Palantir's experience, making the task of catching up to Palantir particularly difficult.

Valuation and Long Game

One of the most common criticisms facing Palantir as a stock is what many view as an absurdly high valuation. However, this valuation is well-deserved given what Palantir could become. That is, Palantir has a real chance of becoming the default operating system for AI-powered enterprises and governments. By traditional metrics, Palantir's shares might seem expensive. For instance, Palantir has an eye-popping forward P/E of ~150 at its current valuation of ~$199B. Not only is this P/E high, but it is basically unheard of for a company of Palantir's size.

However, if Palantir can hit its $3.75 billion in 2025 revenue forecast and continue to grow at roughly 30% annually, which is not only likely but possible given its lead in such a high-growth industry, Palantir's earnings power in 5-10 years could dwarf its current size. In addition, a premium multiple can be justified today as much of its growth will convert to free cash flow given its high software margins. Unlike many AI peers that are still burning through cash, Palantir is scaling up revenue and cash flow at an incredibly fast rate. If Palantir can sustain its exponentially improving data flywheel loop, the company offers investors a rare chance to own a foundational piece of the next era of AI-enabled infrastructure.

data.title: Trump Tariffs Drama: Staples Companies To The Rescue?
data.description:
Introduction

President Trump yesterday announced a new set of tariffs, including reciprocal tariffs, to address the nation's deficit and escalating debt service spending (Figure 1) and improve America's competitive advantage by re-shoring its manufacturing base and reducing dependence on foreign economies. The intent of the tariffs that Trump announced on "Liberation Day" seems straightforward, although it is clear that this strategy will bring significant short-term (and potentially longer-term) pain.

The tariffs, detailed in a fact sheet released by the White House, will almost certainly lead to a rebound in inflation and an increase in the unemployment rate. While the unemployment rate is still near historic lows (Figure 2), the charge-off rate on consumer loans - now well above post-Great Recession levels (Figure 3) - points to an increasingly weak consumer. A more in-depth discussion of this matter can be found in this article.

Clearly, the Trump administration is risking a recession of potentially global proportions with this policy, which is also underlined by the deep red U.S. stock market futures yesterday after the close and by the well-known GDPNow forecasting model of the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta. The indicator currently estimates a quarterly percentage change of almost -4%, and even when adjusted for gold imports and exports, the "nowcast" is still -1.4%.

While there is likely to be a negative impact on most sectors as the tariffs come into force, it seems reasonable to conclude that stocks of companies with a relatively recession-resistant business model, such as consumer staples, are the place to hide from the volatility.

But is this really true, and equally importantly, is it a good idea to prioritize sector allocation to this "safe haven" given the current uncertainties? After all, agreeing with the consensus usually has its price, and such a pro-cyclical investing behavior quickly leads to little or no alpha.

While I am relatively heavily exposed to consumer staples, I am generally a contrarian value investor, so I see the current environment as an opportunity. In my previous articles, for example, I have explained in detail why I have a constructive long-term view on well-capitalized U.S. banks, defense companies and healthcare providers despite the current budget-related issues.

In this article, however, I would like to focus on the safe-haven consumer staples sector mentioned above and explain how I approach investing in this sector in light of President Trump's economic policies. While I don't think it's a good idea to blindly buy a consumer staples-focused ETF or a basket of individual stocks based on well-known investing rationales, it pays off to take a closer look at the following four categories:

  • Consumer Packaged Goods Companies
  • Non-Alcoholic Beverages And Snacks Companies
  • Alcoholic Beverages Companies
  • Tobacco Companies.

1) Consumer Packaged Goods Companies

Shares of consumer packaged goods companies like The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) or Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL) are rarely cheap, especially when uncertainty is high and investors are flocking to risk-off investments. Procter & Gamble and Colgate-Palmolive are companies that I have covered in the past here on Seeking Alpha, discussing their fundamentals and prospects in detail.

With their internationally diversified operations and supply chains, these companies should only be marginally impacted by tariffs. Boycotts, such as those currently seen with Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), are an unrealistic expectation for these companies, even though they are U.S.-based companies. I'm sure many consumers would not be able to name the parent company of the branded product they use every day.

Take, for example, the FAST Graphs chart for PG stock (Figure 4). Although the company has shown strong earnings growth since its restructuring in the mid-2010s, it's important to realize that PG currently trades at 25 times adjusted earnings, while earnings have only grown at about 5% per year over the past 20 years, including analysts' expectations for the next two years.

This equates to a very lofty price-to-earnings-to-growth ratio of 5. If PG stock were to return to its long-term average P/E ratio of 20 over the next two years, the stock would essentially be dead money, notably including the return from the growing dividend. Of course, there is a lot to like with Procter & Gamble's, such as its low debt, Aa3 credit rating with a stable outlook and excellent corporate governance, but of course, also its leading portfolio of everyday brands. Importantly, many of the products sold by P&G are regularly replaced and cost comparatively little in dollar terms, so inflation is not felt as much as with more expensive products. This gives the company a strong position in terms of pricing power.

These attributes naturally also apply to Colgate-Palmolive, including from the point of view of creditworthiness and corporate governance. It is, therefore, not surprising that CL shares are trading at a similarly lofty level:

Given that the market has granted CL stock a higher premium over the past two decades, the expected returns are at least somewhat reasonable at 3.6% per year, according to FAST Graphs. While I think Colgate's prospects are quite solid, as detailed in my last article, I don't see why the company should be trading at a 23x earnings multiple. With a current dividend yield of just 2.1%, the stock also looks unattractive from an income perspective, especially when considering the average dividend growth rate of just 3.3% over the last 10 years. At this rate, it would take roughly 20 years for CL's yield to exceed the current yield on long-term U.S. government bonds.

All in all, consumer packaged goods companies obviously offer shelter from the storm, at least in times of heightened volatility like today. However, I would not seek to "improve my shelter" at the current premium paid.

So when would I buy safe haven stocks like PG or CL?

A period characterized by persistently high inflation combined with comparatively high interest rates is a scenario where consumer goods companies should be trading at buyable valuations. Add to that the narrative of a large incumbent company with a wide range of white-label offerings that could hurt sales of the predominantly branded products, and I would expect really compelling valuations where I could see myself aggressively adding to my holdings in this segment.

One company in this sector that I have written a bullish article about not too long ago is Reckitt Benckiser Group p.l.c. (OTCPK:RBGLY) (OTCPK:RBGPF). Although the stock has shaken off most of the overhang from the infant formula litigation, it still trades at only about 15 times earnings (Figure 6). Admittedly, previous management made some significant mistakes and as a U.K. company it does not enjoy the same appreciation as U.S.-based consumer staples companies. However, its margin profile, improving return on invested capital and gradually de-risked balance sheet (currently rated A3 with a stable outlook) give me confidence in the company's future. I opened a position last year when the market was very pessimistic about Reckitt's litigation risks, but even today I still think the stock deserves a closer look and I might buy a few shares here and there.

2) Non-Alcoholic Beverages And Snacks Companies

Non-alcoholic beverage and snack companies such as PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP), The Coca-Cola Company (KO) and General Mills, Inc. (GIS) fall basically into the same category as the companies described above. As such, they rarely trade at a discount, and I don't expect any significant headwinds from tariffs going forward.

One could argue that a company like Coca-Cola could face boycotts due to the brand's obvious association with America, but I doubt the impact would be significant. After all, the company is deeply entrenched in each market with local suppliers and bottlers. With PepsiCo and General Mills, I see it similarly to Procter & Gamble - many of the brands owned by the company are not directly associated with the parent company, with the soft drink Pepsi-Cola of course being an exception.

Coca-Cola stock exhibits a similar pattern to PG stock (Figure 7), and at a blended P/E ratio of 25 currently, I believe it also falls into the "buying umbrellas during rain" bucket. However, given the company's strong margin profile, its leading brands and its healthy balance sheet (A1 rating with stable outlook), as discussed in detail in my still relevant comparative analysis with PepsiCo, I believe KO stock deserves a premium valuation. I occasionally buy shares of the company and so far, the "buy the dip" mentality has served me well. Selling cash-secured puts during periods of heightened volatility, such as late in 2024, can provide compelling incremental returns. However, with a quality company like Coca-Cola, one has to be careful not to "chase" the stock with selling puts and never end up with an actual position.

I believe that PepsiCo is slightly more impacted by the tariffs than KO due to its different business approach and resulting weaker margin profile. Given that inflation is likely to pick up as a result of the tariffs, a resurgence of volume-related headwinds for companies operating in this sector seems likely, although I would not over-interpret the risk due to the typically low $ value of products offered. Over time, as consumers get used to the status quo on prices, consumer staples companies should have no problem returning to volume growth. Similarly, I doubt there will emerge a secular trend to private label brands, mainly because input cost inflation is affecting the entire industry and the margin profile of companies like KO, as well as PEP and GIS, gives them more room to temporarily absorb higher costs.

Moody's has assigned PepsiCo a long-term rating of A1 with a stable outlook, but I personally consider the company's balance sheet to be slightly worse than Coca-Cola's, especially in light of a very high dividend payout ratio (based on free cash flow). PepsiCo's capex is currently comparatively high due to growth-related investments in the U.S. and abroad, so the payout ratio should decline over time, while earnings (and free cash flow growth) should improve going forward.

In my view, the current somewhat muted near-term growth outlook, high payout ratio, and overhang from potential regulations related to ingredients in the wake of RFK Jr.'s confirmation as Secretary of Health and Human Services (HHS) are the primary reasons for PepsiCo stock's weak performance. PEP typically trades at a price-to-earnings ratio of 21, which I believe is reasonable in a long-term low interest rate environment.

While I don't think the quality of PepsiCo is comparable to P&G, PepsiCo is a good quality company that can be bought at a reasonable valuation right now and faces manageable challenges. With a current price-to-earnings ratio of 18 (Figure 8) and a dividend yield almost as high as longer-term government bonds, I feel comfortable adding to my long-term position here and there.

3) Alcoholic Beverages Companies

The rationale for investing in spirits companies is simple: in difficult economic times, alcohol consumption will increase and spirits companies will flourish. However, as the current performance of leading spirits companies such as Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.A) (BF.B), Diageo p.l.c. (DEO) (OTCPK:DGEAF) or Constellation Brands, Inc. (STZ) shows, this is currently not the case:

To be fair, all of the companies mentioned are suffering from secondary effects related to the lockdown measures imposed in response to the pandemic, but it cannot be denied that consumer trends - especially among younger adults - are showing signs of weakness and lower disposable income due to inflation is also playing its part in the current rather difficult demand environment.

Mexico is not included in the table listing Trump's reciprocal tariffs, and USMCA-compliant goods (Mexico-U.S.-Canada Agreement) remain unaffected. However, as tariffs are a central pillar of Trump's negotiating strategy with the leaders of foreign economies, a related impact on the sales and earnings of companies in the alcohol industry should definitely be factored into the equation.

Mexico was responsible for 7% of Brown-Forman's net sales in FY 2024 (p. 6, FY 2024 10-K), and given the company's tequila distillation activities in Mexico, a headwind to earnings seems likely. In this context, I expect Constellation Brands to be much more impacted in the event of potential future tariffs, as more than 80% of its long-lived tangible assets are located outside the U.S. and primarily in Mexico (p. 110, FY 2024 10-K).

Unlike the low-$ products of Coca-Cola, PepsiCo or Procter & Gamble, the products sold by Brown-Forman and its competitors (with the exception of ready-to-drink cocktails) are much more scrutinized by consumers when prices are increased. Regardless of whether countries such as Mexico are included in the list of tariffs, the expected rebound in inflation when the new tariffs come into force soon will most certainly put further pressure on consumers' disposable income for precisely this reason.

As a result, I am rather cautious on this sector. With a price/earnings ratio of 19, BF.B shares are certainly not trading at a huge discount and given the long-term average earnings growth of 6.7% per annum (Figure 10), I would not rule out further downside.

However, I believe that a lot has been priced in over the last few months. On a deep red stock market day like today, I think it's a pretty telling sign of strength that Brown-Forman shares are down only 3% at the time of writing. And while I think it's unreasonable to expect a return to the long-term average P/E ratio of 27, I consider 20 times earnings a reasonable long-term average valuation for Brown-Forman - as outlined in my cautiously optimistic article from February. Given the current heightened volatility, I am currently short puts with a strike of $30 and $25, but could also see myself buying a few shares here and there directly at around $30.

However, the narrative that stocks of spirits companies are a good place to hide in a recession (which is looking increasingly likely) should be critically questioned. While I concede that much of the underperformance of companies in this sector is due to the correction of a multi-year overshoot, the comparatively high prices of branded spirits make this sector more cyclical than first thought. Therefore, the pricing power of these companies in times of high inflation should not be over-interpreted. Although I have a constructive long-term view on Brown-Forman and Diageo, I am therefore keeping my exposure limited and only feel comfortable holding these positions in a broadly diversified portfolio.

4) Tobacco Companies

In principle, the same investment rationale applies to tobacco companies as to companies that produce alcoholic beverages. However, given the still comparatively high affordability of cigarettes (especially in the U.S.), I think it has much more merit for tobacco companies.

A tariff-induced earnings headwind due to partial dependence on imported tobacco makes sense, at least in theory. Here, companies that only sell their products in the U.S. (Altria Group, Inc., (MO)), or that are heavily exposed to the U.S. like British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI) (OTCPK:BTAFF), seem to be the most vulnerable, but investors should consider this risk in light of the pricing power and renowned margin profile of tobacco companies. Personally, I would classify the associated headwinds as insignificant. Conversely, higher tariffs on goods imported from China or possibly even a targeted crackdown on imported illegal vapor products, heated tobacco and oral nicotine products could be seen as a tailwind for these companies.

I write fairly regularly here on Seeking Alpha about tobacco companies, which make up a significant part of my portfolio. For example, I've weighed the pros and cons of a Trump election victory and the secondary effects it would have on British American Tobacco and Altria Group. While I have rated both stocks as buys in my earlier articles, as well as the market leader in smoke-free alternatives, Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), and smaller competitor Imperial Brands p.l.c. (OTCQX:IMBBY) (OTCQX:IMBBF), I am personally no longer adding to my tobacco positions given the significant gains over the course of 2024.

Of course, dividend yields of 5% to 7% appear still highly attractive and with P/E ratios of 11 and 9, both MO shares and BTI ADRs look cheaply valued (Figure 11 and Figure 12). Imperial also looks favorable with a P/E ratio of 9 (Figure 13), and the clear relative outperformance these days once again qualifies tobacco companies as portfolio hedges due to their often inverse correlation with the broad market.

Despite the significant outperformance, I would not hedge my portfolio with tobacco stocks today. After all, it's important to remember that the core business of these companies remains in decline, and a key part of my investment thesis in Imperial Brands, for example, was to buy the stock when the market actually priced it for terminal decline (see this article). And while I commend these companies for their relentless focus on offsetting volume declines with price increases, but more importantly driving operational efficiency gains, the headroom seems rather limited given the increasing price elasticity of demand and already very high margins.

The obvious exception is, of course, Philip Morris International. However, with a price/earnings ratio of 23 (Figure 14), I think it is fairly obvious that the market is once again accepting the company as a "risk-off" consumer staples investment. Therefore, I am not currently planning to add to my (already sizable) position.

At the same time, one could argue that tobacco stocks could be sold, and the proceeds opportunistically reinvested in contrarian ideas such as those mentioned in the introduction. However, given the very significant yields on costs of my tobacco positions, I think it is a much better approach to simply not reinvest the dividends and redeploy the funds elsewhere.

Conclusion

Even if the intentions communicated with the announcement of the reciprocal tariffs are in America's best interest, I'm pretty confident that things will get significantly worse before they get better. As I discussed in one of my previous articles, one of the big drawbacks of such negotiating tactics is the resulting difficulty for companies to plan, which can lead to lethargic behavior in terms of investing. This can quickly turn into a vicious circle of significantly higher unemployment, long-term stagflation, and a further erosion of middle-class prosperity.

Initial reactions to Trump's "Liberation Day" speech have been as expected - both China and the European Union have announced retaliatory measures in response to the announcement. So it's pretty clear that things will most likely get worse before they get better. The trade war has reached a new level, and it will take some time before the trading partners (who in many cases are more interdependent than admitted) can agree on reasonable terms again.

Rebalancing the portfolio towards non-cyclical or only moderately cyclical companies in the consumer staples sector seems like a good idea to dodge this bullet. However, as I have shown in this article, the companies that offer real protection from the storm are usually priced accordingly. I currently see no reason to add to my positions in the consumer packaged goods sector (with the exception of a few shares in Reckitt Benckiser Group here or there), while the soft drinks and snacks segment offers (modest) value. PepsiCo and General Mills are two companies I am currently adding to, albeit far from aggressively.

Buying shares in companies that produce alcoholic beverages also sounds like a good idea due to the obvious narrative, but on closer inspection, the sector is quite risky. The pricing power of companies focused on spirits in particular is limited, making them likely underperformers in a stagflationary environment. However, in the context of a diversified consumer staples portfolio and a long-term investment horizon, I feel comfortable being exposed to Brown-Forman shares via short puts (and potentially being assigned the shares). After all, the company is currently as cheaply valued as it was during the Great Recession.

Finally, tobacco stocks also appear to be good candidates for the current risk-off environment due to their high dividend yields and a similar investment rationale to alcoholic beverages companies. Unlike spirits companies, however, I doubt that tobacco companies will face significant tariff-related headwinds. Importantly, given the still fairly high affordability of cigarettes in the U.S., the comparatively strong pricing power of companies in the sector, and potentially temporarily stabilizing volumes in a recession (as in 2020), tobacco stocks should hold up well.

However, I think that a lot has already been priced in during 2024 and the sector-specific risks should not be ignored either. As per my previous articles, I maintain that the best time to buy tobacco companies is when the market is pricing them for terminal decline.

Finally, a word of serenity: politically driven market environments do not last forever, and investors with a broadly diversified portfolio should not lose sleep over yesterday's announcements. Even though the probability of a recession has increased significantly, several indicators are still far from alarming, such as the yield spread between 10-year Treasuries and Baa corporate bonds.

Personally, I approach the current environment with my usual long-term view and regularly buy shares in companies with staying power, a tangible economic moat and a solid financial foundation - regardless of the short-term headwinds associated with the tariffs because: this too shall pass.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariffs On, Risk Off: Credit Set To Leak
data.description:
By Timothy Rahill, & Jeroen van den Broek

Tariffs and technicals

Spreads are widening further and are expected to experience increased volatility due to the announced tariffs. The risk-off sentiment is likely to persist, leading to a slowdown in primary market activity during the upcoming volatile sessions. Within the credit space, many corporates and sectors will feel the pinch, looking more at the macro/rates environment and its focus on the negative impact on eurozone growth. Spreads have already opened up wider as sellers dominate. The iTraxx main has widened by 3bp this morning, now over 10bp wider compared to a couple of weeks ago.

The technical picture is shifting more negatively with outflows, high CSPP redemptions and potentially higher supply (via more Reverse Yankee supply and tariffs absorbing margins, weakening fundamentals), all adding pressure on spreads. And with a lesser dominant demand from yield to soften the blow.

Technical strength is showing signs of weakening, with another week of negative flows from mutual funds and ETFs. The changing rates environment, where the short end is unlikely to see further reductions and the long end is expected to rise, is diminishing the appeal of yield in credit. This is particularly concerning as the bund/rate component of the yield in credit is over 80%.

EUR IG non-financials spread developments and range

The blue indicates when we published the recent piece 'Where’s the credit?' where we suggested it may be time to sell credit

USD has already underperformed EUR

USD credit was a notable underperformer throughout March, with significant widening versus EUR credit. Although USD credit was coming from a very tight and rich place, it still looks a touch on the expensive side, in our opinion. The yield play may still be favourable in USD as rates could be set for some lowering, as detailed in the report Market reaction to Liberation day - this is just the beginning, but further widening is likely on the cards for USD credit longer term.

Regardless, this recent widening of USD spreads versus EUR does offer a larger potential cost-saving advantage for US issuers to come to the EUR market with Reverse Yankee bonds, particularly considering how low the cross currency basis swap is. The supply pressure on EUR credit adds another piece to the deteriorating technical puzzle.

Short end preferred and plenty to avoid

A conservative positioning is advisable, as higher beta and lower-rated debt are poised to underperform from these compressed levels. We also prefer shorter maturities on the curve, anticipating further steepening. Last week, only the 0-4 year maturity bucket saw inflows. We expect higher beta and lower-rated debt to underperform on the long end, with more underperformance likely as curves remain flat.

Within European corporates, consumer, pharma, technology, and industrials sectors have the largest exposure in terms of revenues from the US. Similarly, corporates from the Netherlands, Germany, France and Spain see the most exposure of US revenues. Italy is also noteworthy, considering the large manufacturing sectors that will be impacted.

The sectors that are going to feel the pinch the most and see credit underperfomance are:

  • Autos
  • Pharmaceuticals
  • Food & Bev
  • Airlines
  • Chemicals
  • Steel & Aluminium
  • Manufacturing and other Industrials.

Whilst these tariffs offer more volatility and underperformance of certain segments in the coming sessions, in the medium term we expect spreads will continue a slow leakage wider. ASW spreads could temporarily even pierce the wide end of the range.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
